narrative: Dear [Student's Name], as a Black woman applying to law school, you've poured your heart into this journey, and we appreciate the strength it takes to pursue your dreams in a field that needs more voices like yours. Your profile includes an undergraduate GPA of 3.00, an LSAT score of 34, upper-middle family income, full-time student status, and school deciles of 4th in Year 1 and 2nd in Year 3 based on your grades. The admissions model, which predicts first-time bar exam success using patterns from thousands of similar students, estimated a 71.8% chance that you would fail the bar—high enough to lead to the rejection of your application, as the school prioritizes those likely to pass on the first try. The biggest factor pushing toward this failure prediction was your Year 3 decile ranking of 2nd, which is notably lower than the typical student's 5.53 average and signaled potential challenges in sustaining strong performance. Your undergraduate GPA of 3.00, below the dataset average of 3.21, had the next strongest influence but actually nudged the prediction slightly toward passing, showing it wasn't as detrimental as it might seem. Meanwhile, your upper-middle family income contributed toward the failure prediction compared to typical students, perhaps reflecting certain patterns the model observed. Your Year 1 decile of 4th (versus an average of 5.71), LSAT score, and full-time status had minimal impact overall. Compared to typical students who pass—who often have higher deciles, GPAs around 3.21, and LSATs near 36.7—your Year 3 performance stood out as the key gap. This isn't a judgment on your potential, but a data-driven call based on these patterns. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT or strengthening your recent grades through additional coursework, then reapply to schools with holistic reviews that value your unique background. Programs offering bar prep support for underrepresented students could also boost your odds. You've got resilience—use this as fuel to rewrite your story. We're rooting for you.
